story.headline: Texas steamrolls UCLA, 55-20
body: Texas dominated from start to finish in a 55-20 rout over UCLA in Week 3 PCFL action, with Arch Manning earning Player of the Game honors after going for 27 of 40 for 378 yards and 5 TDs.
The Longhorns outgained UCLA 510–322 in total offense, including 137 yards on the ground. UCLA kept fighting, managing 322 total yards on the night.
Up next, Texas hosts Boston College in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Manning's arm too much as Texas overwhelms UCLA in Week Three blowout
body: Arch Manning put on a surgical passing display that UCLA's secondary simply could not contain, completing 27 of 40 attempts for 378 yards and five touchdowns to steer Texas to a dominant 55-20 victory. The quarterback's efficiency—nearly matching UCLA's entire passing output alone—left the Bruins defense grasping for answers from the opening drive onward. Manning was precise in the pocket and patient with his progressions, letting plays develop rather than forcing into tight windows, which proved the formula UCLA's coverage schemes had no remedy for.

Texas's ground game remained a secondary concern for much of the afternoon despite the team's 137 rushing yards. UCLA's run defense, though allowing 122 net yards more than it surrendered, bent but didn't truly break in the trenches. The real damage was systemic: once Manning established confidence in the air, the Bruins' linebackers and safeties were caught leaning, unable to balance the threat. Iamaleava kept UCLA competitive on the scoreboard with three touchdown passes of his own, but his team's 15 rushing yards painted the fuller picture of a lopsided afternoon.

Texas's ability to win the turnover battle by limiting mistakes—one fumble lost to UCLA's one—while forcing nothing speaks to composure and execution. The Bruins had to chase points from early in the second quarter onward, which compressed their offensive rhythm and left their staff few opportunities to establish rhythm on the ground. By the time UCLA adjusted tactically, the deficit was already insurmountable, and Manning had hit his stride.
aiStory.turningPoint: Manning's first two touchdown passes in the second quarter transformed what began as a competitive game into a rout, as UCLA's secondary visibly lost confidence in coverage and began surrendering space over the middle.

unsungHero.name: Anderson
unsungHero.why: Five receptions for 74 yards anchored Texas's receiving depth and forced UCLA to account for multiple threats, yet he didn't find the end zone like others around him did, keeping his performance out of the spotlight despite consistent, clutch availability.

story.headline: Tennessee holds off Ohio State, 35-29
body: Tennessee found just enough in a 35-29 win over Ohio State in Week 3 PCFL action, with Daune Morris earning Player of the Game honors.
The Volunteers outgained Ohio State 446–351 in total offense, including 105 yards on the ground. Ohio State kept fighting, managing 351 total yards on the night.
Up next, Tennessee hosts Arkansas in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: MacIntyre leads Tennessee past Ohio State in passing duel
body: Tennessee's air attack overwhelmed Ohio State's secondary in a Week 3 shootout that came down to execution in critical moments. MacIntyre completed 31 of 49 passes for 346 yards and three touchdowns, establishing early rhythm through the middle of the field and forcing the Ohio State defense into reactive coverage. The Buckeyes countered with Sayin's efficient if limited performance—25 for 40, 259 yards, one touchdown—but couldn't generate enough explosive plays to match Tennessee's sustained drive-and-score approach.

The turning point arrived in the third quarter when Tennessee's defense tightened coverage on Ohio State's receivers, particularly harassing Sayin on third and long situations. Smith's eight receptions for 110 yards gave Tennessee a reliable safety valve that Ohio State's Kelly and the secondary simply couldn't account for on every snap. By the final quarter, Tennessee had established enough of a cushion that even when Ohio State's defense stiffened, the Volunteers' lead held firm. The decisive margin—six points—reflected two offenses that executed in patches but never found complete rhythm simultaneously.

Tennessee's possession management and superior passing efficiency ultimately separated the teams. The Volunteers controlled time of possession through MacIntyre's willingness to take what the coverage gave him, while Ohio State's run game never found traction despite Judkins' touchdown. Tennessee's one turnover proved inconsequential; Ohio State's turnover-free performance couldn't compensate for falling short on third-down conversions and explosive pass plays. This was a game where the team that executed the passing game with greater consistency won on the road in a manner that felt convincing despite the final score's tightness.
aiStory.turningPoint: Tennessee's secondary forced Sayin into uncomfortable throwing windows in the third quarter, triggering a run of three-and-outs that let the Volunteers extend their lead to double digits.

unsungHero.name: WR Smith (Tennessee)
unsungHero.why: Smith's eight catches for 110 yards provided MacIntyre's most reliable progression option and forced Ohio State into predictable coverage rotations, but Daune Morris earned POG honors despite Bishop's heavier workload overshadowing the receiving corps that opened lanes.

story.headline: Notre Dame takes control late, tops Georgia 41-28
body: Notre Dame asserted control when it mattered in a 41-28 win over Georgia in Week 3 PCFL action, with CJ Carr earning Player of the Game honors after going for 28 of 42 for 412 yards and 3 TDs.
The Fighting Irish outgained Georgia 551–361 in total offense, including 149 yards on the ground. Georgia kept fighting, managing 361 total yards on the night.
Up next, Notre Dame hosts Oregon in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Notre Dame's Carr takes down Georgia in offensive shootout
body: CJ Carr orchestrated a clinic in the passing game, completing 28 of 42 attempts for 412 yards and three touchdowns to lead Notre Dame past Georgia 41-28 in a Week 3 matchup that showcased two capable offensive systems unable to finish the same way. Georgia's Giles Gilbert kept pace with his own three touchdown passes and 271 yards through the air, but the Notre Dame quarterback's superior efficiency and command of the rhythm offense made the difference when it mattered. The gap in total yardage, 551 to 361, tells a story of Notre Dame's ability to sustain drives where Georgia could not.

Georgia's defense entered the contest ranked among the stingier units in the league, but Notre Dame's balance kept the unit honest all afternoon. Smothers carried the ball 16 times for 80 yards, not flashy but steady enough to make third downs convertible and keep the Georgia pass rush from settling in. Meanwhile, Carr had time to find receivers like McPherson, who made five receptions for 99 yards despite drawing attention downfield. Georgia's Mondon Jr. recorded four tackles and a sack, his best effort insufficient to shift momentum when it counted.

The turnover battle revealed another layer: Notre Dame coughed up one fumble but recovered the fumble with it, erasing its one mistake. Georgia committed nothing but also generated nothing in the explosive moments that swing close games. Young's six receptions and 108 yards kept the passing chains alive for Georgia, yet the volume required to stay competitive worked against clock management and field position. By the fourth quarter, Notre Dame had the breathing room Giles and his offense never quite found.
aiStory.turningPoint: Notre Dame's sustained drive in the third quarter, where Carr moved the ball methodically without taking negative plays, pushed the lead to double digits while Georgia's defense grew tentative.

unsungHero.name: Smothers
unsungHero.why: Sixteen carries might not leap off the stat sheet, but grinding out 80 yards kept Georgia's front seven honest and allowed Carr to operate in rhythm without needing hero throws on every snap.

story.headline: LSU outlasts Michigan in 30-24 battle
body: LSU found just enough in a 30-24 win over Michigan in Week 3 PCFL action, with Garrett Nussmeier earning Player of the Game honors after going for 22 of 38 for 275 yards and 2 TDs.
The Tigers were outgained by Michigan 456–442 in total offense, including 178 yards on the ground. Michigan kept fighting, managing 456 total yards on the night.
Up next, LSU hosts Penn State in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Nussmeier's poise carries LSU past Michigan in defensive stalemate
body: LSU's Garrett Nussmeier overcame an ugly first half to deliver when it mattered most, steering the Tigers to a 30-24 victory over Michigan in a game that turned on field position and composure rather than explosive plays. Nussmeier completed 22 of 38 passes for 275 yards and two touchdowns, never forcing a dangerous decision despite Michigan's defense keeping him off schedule throughout the afternoon.

The real battle was fought in the trenches. Michigan outgained LSU by 14 total yards and held a clear advantage in rushing production—Wendtland's 99 yards on nine carries gave the Wolverines a physical edge up front—yet those advantages never translated into sustained scoring drives. LSU's defense, anchored by Mangum's seven tackles, forced Michigan into uncomfortable third-and-long situations repeatedly, while the Tigers' rushing attack, though modest at 178 yards, moved the chains when LSU needed six.

Hasty's 79-yard receiving performance proved the difference in tight moments. The wideout's ability to create separation gave Nussmeier a consistent outlet, and his 79 yards effectively neutralized Michigan's secondary depth issues. Gabriel managed only 203 yards for Michigan despite matching Nussmeier in completions, a sign the Tigers' pass rush periodically disrupted timing and forced hurried releases that limited the Wolverines' vertical game.

Neither offense was flawless. The absence of turnovers from both teams underscored how carefully each side operated, but LSU's slight edge in explosive plays—particularly touchdown passes to Hasty and Ferguson—separated a close game that could have swung on a single sequence.
aiStory.turningPoint: Ferguson's 1-yard touchdown run late in the third quarter gave LSU a two-score lead at a moment when Michigan was gaining momentum behind Wendtland's ground game, forcing the Wolverines into a pass-heavy final quarter that played to LSU's defensive strengths.

unsungHero.name: Mangum
unsungHero.why: His seven tackles and relentless effort in coverage allowed LSU's secondary to operate safely while limiting Michigan's ability to establish rhythm offensively, yet the stat sheet doesn't capture how many times he forced early incompletions or kills plays before they developed.

story.headline: Boston College takes control late, tops Colorado 39-23
body: Boston College asserted control when it mattered in a 39-23 win over Colorado in Week 3 PCFL action, with Bo MacCormick earning Player of the Game honors after going for 1 catches for 59 yards.
The Eagles outgained Colorado 450–210 in total offense, including 126 yards on the ground. Colorado kept fighting, managing 210 total yards on the night.
Up next, Boston College travels to face Texas in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Boston College's ground game overwhelms Colorado in lopsided Week 3 affair
body: Boston College's offense dictated terms from the opening drive, establishing a 126-yard rushing advantage that Colorado's defense simply could not match. The Eagles controlled the line of scrimmage throughout the afternoon, forcing Colorado into predictable passing situations and preventing the Buffaloes from establishing any sustained rhythm on offense. Quarterback Leavitt distributed the ball efficiently across 27 completions, finding open receivers downfield while the run game kept chains moving and the clock ticking in Boston College's favor.

Colorado's passing attack, led by Anderson's 18-for-43 effort, revealed the cost of playing from behind. The Buffaloes managed two touchdown passes but operated in reactive mode for most of the game, unable to generate the yardage or efficiency needed to keep pace. Van Horne's three carries for six yards underscored the reality: Colorado's running back could not find creases, and that inability to threaten on the ground left Anderson and the passing game isolated and vulnerable to Boston College's pass rush.

The Eagles' defensive front, anchored by Collins' two sacks, made life uncomfortable for Anderson throughout. Boston College surrendered nothing in the turnover column while Boston College similarly protected the football, a clean exchange that favored the more dominant team. This was control football executed at a level Colorado's staff could not match or answer, a Week 3 statement from a Boston College team that appears to have found its identity.
aiStory.turningPoint: Boston College's opening-quarter establishment of the run game forced Colorado into obvious passing situations, where the Buffaloes' defense became vulnerable to Leavitt's intermediate throws and the Eagles' play-action offense.

unsungHero.name: Mcdonald
unsungHero.why: His 84 yards on 13 carries provided the grinding, consistent yardage that sustained drives and kept Colorado's defense honest, even though MacCormick's single explosive reception stole the Player of the Game honor.

story.headline: Arkansas steamrolls Penn State, 41-18
body: Arkansas dominated from start to finish in a 41-18 rout over Penn State in Week 3 PCFL action, with Cade Trotter earning Player of the Game honors after going for 15 of 25 for 232 yards and 3 TDs.
The Razorbacks outgained Penn State 408–401 in total offense, including 110 yards on the ground. Penn State kept fighting, managing 401 total yards on the night.
Up next, Arkansas travels to face Tennessee in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Arkansas Survives Efficient Penn State Passing Attack to Win 41-18
body: Arkansas' offense found its rhythm early and never relinquished it, with Cade Trotter orchestrating a precision passing attack that punished Penn State's secondary despite completing just 60 percent of his throws. Trotter's three touchdown passes, all to different receivers, reflected an Arkansas scheme that kept the Penn State defense honest and unable to gang up on any one threat. The Razorbacks' ground game added balance—Washington's 83 rushing yards and one score on just five carries suggested explosive plays when Arkansas needed them most.

Penn State's QB Myers put together a respectable day by volume, completing 26 of 50 passes for 338 yards and a touchdown to Edwards, who hauled in 119 yards on six receptions. The problem was efficiency and rhythm. Penn State's running game mustered only 75 yards, forcing Myers into a pass-heavy approach where Arkansas' secondary could sit and react. The Nittany Lions moved the ball enough to stay competitive into the third quarter, but couldn't sustain drives when they mattered most.

The decisive gap was Arkansas' ability to finish. While Penn State's yards-per-play looked respectable on a statistical sheet, the Razorbacks converted opportunities into points—three scoring drives in the first half established a 24-11 halftime lead that Penn State simply couldn't erode. Trotter's efficiency in the red zone, combined with Arkansas' willingness to lean on the run when it counted, proved the difference between two offenses that moved the football with relative ease.
aiStory.turningPoint: Arkansas scored touchdowns on three of its first four drives before halftime, jumping to a 24-11 lead that Penn State's second-half adjustments couldn't overcome.

unsungHero.name: WR Wright
unsungHero.why: Wright's 140 yards and two touchdowns were the primary target for Trotter's precision passing, yet the Player of the Game nod went to Trotter despite Wright essentially being his favorite option all evening.

story.headline: Oregon survives Washington by 1 in thriller
body: Oregon found just enough in a 35-34 thriller over Washington in Week 3 PCFL action, with Brock Thomas earning Player of the Game honors after going for 19 of 27 for 273 yards and 5 TDs.
The Ducks outgained Washington 364–345 in total offense, including 109 yards on the ground. Washington kept fighting, managing 345 total yards on the night.
Up next, Oregon travels to face Notre Dame in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Oregon Survives Washington Turnover Chaos in Thriller
body: Oregon escaped Eugene with a one-point victory over Washington in a game that hinged less on execution than on mistake management. The Ducks won 35-34 despite being outrushed 146 to 109, a gap that underscores how thoroughly Brock Thomas and the passing game took over when it mattered. Thomas completed 19 of 27 passes for 273 yards and five touchdowns, never asked to do too much, never forced into uncomfortable positions. He was efficient and lethal in equal measure.

The decisive flaw belonged entirely to Washington. Three fumbles to Oregon's zero created a turnover margin that proved insurmountable even when the Huskies showed genuine offensive competence. Williams Jr. moved the ball effectively, connecting with the receiver Williams nine times for 92 yards and two touchdowns. Limar added a touchdown on the ground. The pieces were there. The execution was there. What wasn't there was ball security. Those three lost fumbles cost Washington possessions in territory where points were within reach.

Oregon's defense, anchored by Obidegwu's seven tackles, bent without breaking. They gave up 345 total yards and allowed Washington to score 34 points, yet they made the one play that mattered: forcing turnovers and keeping their offense on the field long enough for Thomas to orchestrate the go-ahead scores. In a game decided by a single point, that margin between caution and carelessness proved to be everything.
aiStory.turningPoint: Washington's third fumble in the fourth quarter, which Oregon recovered and converted into a touchdown drive, eliminated any realistic chance of a Husky comeback.

unsungHero.name: Pellum
unsungHero.why: Six catches for 98 yards on a night when Thomas distributed the ball across multiple receivers, Pellum was the consistent intermediate threat who moved the chains when Oregon needed tempo and rhythm.

story.headline: Miami outlasts USC in 34-28 battle
body: Miami found just enough in a 34-28 win over USC in Week 3 PCFL action, with Graham Mertz earning Player of the Game honors after going for 41 of 67 for 418 yards and 2 TDs.
The Hurricanes outgained USC 488–394 in total offense, including 80 yards on the ground. USC kept fighting, managing 394 total yards on the night.
Up next, Miami travels to face Georgia in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Miami's Air Attack Overwhelms USC in High-Scoring Week 3 Shootout
body: Graham Mertz orchestrated a clinic in controlled aggression, completing 41 of 67 passes for 418 yards and two touchdowns to lead Miami to a 34-28 victory over USC on Saturday. The Hurricanes' quarterback thrived in the up-tempo game, finding soft spots in USC's coverage and consistently moving the chains when the moment demanded it. Though Mertz threw the one interception of the day, his sheer volume and precision kept Miami's offense humming through four quarters.

USC's Moss put together a respectable afternoon with 315 passing yards and two scores, but the Trojans' ground game never materialized. Marks managed only 22 yards on 13 carries, forcing USC into predictable passing situations that Miami's secondary eventually learned to read. The Hurricanes' pass rush, while not dominant on the stat sheet, applied enough consistent pressure to disrupt timing and force Moss into uncomfortable decisions. Miami's Lilly added a 57-yard rushing effort and one touchdown, a balanced contribution that kept the Trojans' defense honest.

WR Mack provided USC's most explosive moments, hauling in seven catches for 109 yards and proving to be one of the few reliable targets when the Trojans needed yards. Yet even his production couldn't compensate for Miami's depth in the passing game. The Hurricanes' balanced offensive arsenal, combined with Mertz's willingness to stay patient in the pocket and take what the defense gave him, proved the difference in a game that showcased two offensive systems headed in different directions.
aiStory.turningPoint: Miami's ability to sustain drives in the third quarter, particularly through Mertz's accuracy on intermediate routes, allowed the Hurricanes to pull away while USC's offense sputtered and never truly found rhythm after halftime.

unsungHero.name: Wingo
unsungHero.why: Eight tackles and consistent assignment discipline in coverage kept Miami's defense sound despite being on the field frequently. While Mertz earned the spotlight for his prolific passing, Wingo's work in the secondary prevented USC from establishing any explosive plays downfield.

story.headline: Instant classic: Clemson edges Oklahoma, 28-27
body: Clemson found just enough in a 28-27 thriller over Oklahoma in Week 3 PCFL action, with James Cortez earning Player of the Game honors after going for 27 of 37 for 388 yards and 3 TDs.
The Tigers were outgained by Oklahoma 413–397 in total offense, including 21 yards on the ground. Oklahoma kept fighting, managing 413 total yards on the night.
Up next, Clemson travels to face Colorado in Week 4.

aiStory.headline: Clemson survives Oklahoma shootout in turnover-marred thriller
body: Clemson escaped with a 28-27 victory over Oklahoma in a game that turned entirely on ball security. Both offenses moved the field with ease, combining for 784 yards and six touchdown passes, but Oklahoma's three fumbles proved lethal in a one-point decision. Glass found Walsh for 119 yards and a score, and the Oklahoma passing game was crisp enough to keep pace with Clemson's efficient attack, yet those turnovers created a gap neither Glass nor the offense could quite close.

James Cortez orchestrated the winning performance with composure and precision, completing 27 of 37 passes for 388 yards and three touchdowns. His poise in the pocket was the difference maker—he never turned the ball over and avoided the pressure mistakes that plagued Glass. Clemson's defense, anchored by Carter's sack and six tackles, applied just enough resistance to force Oklahoma into mistakes when it mattered most. The Clemson secondary bent but didn't break, holding its ground as Glass aired it out 47 times.

What could have been a showcase offensive clinic became a cautionary tale about turnover margin. Oklahoma dominated the line of scrimmage statistically, gaining 413 total yards to Clemson's 397, and actually out-rushed Clemson 42 to 21. But yardage doesn't account for those three fumbles that shifted momentum at critical junctures. Clemson will leave Norman knowing they won a game they didn't necessarily play better, a margin of luck that won't sustain them long term but proved decisive on this October afternoon.
aiStory.turningPoint: Oklahoma's third fumble in the fourth quarter, deep in Clemson territory with the game hanging in the balance, squandered what could have been a go-ahead score and handed Clemson the final possession it needed.

unsungHero.name: Fields
unsungHero.why: Fields caught six passes for 107 yards and two touchdowns—equaling half of Clemson's passing production—yet Cortez earned Player of the Game because his turnover-free execution defined the victory.

weeklyWrap.headline: Arkansas Shocks Penn State; Tennessee Survives Ohio State Test
weeklyWrap.subhead: Week 3 delivered one stunning result and a narrow escape for the top-ranked team, while Notre Dame's statement win reshapes the upper echelon.
weeklyWrap.lead: Arkansas walked into Penn State and left with a 41-18 victory that stands as the week's most jarring result, forcing an immediate reassessment of where the Nittany Lions truly stand. Meanwhile, Tennessee's one-point survival against Ohio State and Notre Dame's demolition of Georgia have created genuine separation in the power poll's upper reaches, even as the field began to sort itself out.

segments.title: Arkansas Dismantles Penn State on the Road
segments.body: Coming in ranked fourth in the power poll, Arkansas entered Happy Valley and imposed its will entirely, posting 41 points while holding Penn State to 18. The Razorbacks' offensive line controlled the line of scrimmage and their defensive scheme created persistent pressure, suggesting this team has the personnel and depth to sustain a run at the top. Penn State's defensive struggles and inability to establish rhythm on offense raises hard questions about whether the Nittany Lions are overcooked early or simply caught an Arkansas side at full strength.

segments.title: Tennessee Edges Ohio State in Defensive Chess Match
segments.body: Tennessee's 35-29 win over Ohio State was tighter than the final score suggests, but it was the Volunteers' secondary that ultimately made the difference in the closing moments. Ohio State's passing attack generated yards and points throughout, yet Tennessee's cornerbacks tightened coverage in critical third-quarter situations and forced the Buckeyes into uncomfortable field position. For a top-ranked team, Tennessee showed poise and discipline rather than dominance, exactly the kind of grinding road win that builds championship credibility.

segments.title: Notre Dame Dismantles Georgia in Dominant Display
segments.body: Notre Dame's 41-28 victory over Georgia wasn't just a win—it was a comprehensive statement that the Irish belong among the elite tier. The Irish controlled tempo, established the run early, and their defense set the tone from the opening drive, suffocating Georgia's rhythm. This performance vaulted Notre Dame into the top four and confirmed that the Bulldogs, despite their reputation, are vulnerable to teams with superior execution and aggression.

segments.title: Washington and Oregon Trade Blows in Instant Classic
segments.body: Oregon's 35-34 escape over Washington delivered one of the week's most entertaining contests, with both offenses operating at a fever pitch throughout. The Ducks' final drive capped a back-and-forth affair that saw lead changes and big plays on both sides, and Oregon's ability to answer Washington's late push speaks to offensive talent in abundance. The losing effort does little to dim Washington's status as a dangerous team, but Oregon's poise in a shootout environment shows the kind of clutch that separates contenders from the rest.

segments.title: Texas Domination Sets Early Pace in Wide-Open Field
segments.body: Texas' 55-20 dismantling of UCLA wasn't against a powerhouse, but the offensive efficiency and defensive suffocation on display registered a statement nonetheless. The Longhorns sit at three in the power poll and have room to climb if they continue to execute with this level of precision and intensity. In a top tier that now includes Tennessee, LSU, Notre Dame, and Arkansas with varying degrees of proven resistance, Texas' early efficiency gives them a clear path upward if the defense stays disciplined.

weeklyWrap.closer: Week 4 will test whether Tennessee and Notre Dame can maintain their momentum, while Arkansas faces the task of proving last week's dominance wasn't a one-off.
